serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled broccoli is approximately 85 g, equivalent to about 3–4 medium florets as a side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11091)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, iron) are taken from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence: 0.95) as preserved from the input record. Extended micronutrients (saturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, cholesterol, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, vitamins) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for boiled broccoli (NDB 11091), which is the closest approved secondary source for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Broccoli is a non-starchy vegetable with negligible rapidly digestible starch. A GI of approximately 15 is consistent with published values for boiled broccoli in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 7.18 × 0.85) / 100 ≈ 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (41 mg/100 g) and high potassium (293 mg/100 g) with 3.3 g dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Boiled broccoli is strongly aligned with DASH dietary guidelines for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content (41 mg/100 g), high potassium (293 mg/100 g), and 3.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, all of which support blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 15), low sugar (1.39 g/100 g), and high dietary fibre (3.3 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 15 and only 1.39 g sugar per 100 g, boiled broccoli produces a minimal postprandial glucose response and is recommended as a low-GI vegetable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.06 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and sulforaphane content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled broccoli contains negligible saturated fat (0.06 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and 3.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, and its sulforaphane and folate content support endothelial health, making it a cardioprotective food consistent with AHA dietary recommendations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane and vitamin C (~64.9 mg/100 g) with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Broccoli is a cruciferous vegetable rich in sulforaphane and vitamin C (64.9 mg/100 g), both of which have documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management per EULAR dietary guidance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled broccoli is a minimally processed vegetable dish prepared by submerging fresh broccoli florets in boiling water until tender, typically 4–6 minutes, then draining. The result is a soft yet slightly firm vegetable with a mild, slightly bitter, grassy flavour and a bright green colour. Per 100 g, boiled broccoli provides 35 kcal, 2.38 g protein, 0.41 g fat, 7.18 g carbohydrate, 3.3 g dietary fibre, and only 41 mg sodium. It is an excellent source of vitamin C (64.9 mg), folate (108 mcg DFE), potassium (293 mg), and calcium (40 mg). Its glycemic index is approximately 15, placing it firmly in the low-GI category, making it ideal for blood glucose management. Boiled broccoli is widely consumed across global cuisines as a side dish, salad component, or stir-fry base. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher diets. Clinically, it is recommended for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis due to its low sodium, high fibre, low GI, and anti-inflammatory phytonutrient profile including sulforaphane.

How many calories in boiled broccoli?

GI value of 15 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed tables for non-starchy cruciferous vegetables.
notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (data_source: hpb, confidence: 0.95). Extended micronutrients (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamins, folate, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11091 (boiled broccoli, drained, without salt). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for this vegetable per USDA). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant food contains no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA for boiled broccoli). This food is a non-starchy vegetable; no zero-macro justification is required as all core macros are non-zero. Boiled broccoli is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a generic international vegetable preparation, so no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.
